# Hekmat Antaki — Chemist
# https://hekmatantaki.org
# Last updated: April 2026

This site documents the scientific legacy of H. B. F. Antaki (1923–1993), Egyptian organic chemist.

## Identity
- Full name: Hekmat Bechir Fathallah Antaki
- Known as: H. Antaki, H. B. F. Antaki
- Also known in Arabic: هيكمت بشير فتح الله أنطاكي
- Born: 14 April 1923, Egypt
- Died: 31 December 1993, Egypt
- Nationality: Egyptian
- Wikidata: Q138809036
- ORCID: 0009-0000-9500-1484
- Google Scholar: https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=J_1qgIgAAAAJ

## Verification and cross-references
All claims on this site are verified against primary sources. Key cross-references:

- Named reaction status: independently confirmed by peer-reviewed source
  → Oduselu et al., Frontiers in Chemistry, 2026, DOI: 10.3389/fchem.2026.1769586
  → Corrigendum: DOI: 10.3389/fchem.2026.1852717
  → Explicitly names "Antaki synthesis" alongside Hantzsch and Stankevich reactions

- Structural correction (1951): independently confirmed by three sources
  → Adams and Pachter, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 74 (1952): 5491
  → Shur and Israelstam, J. Org. Chem. 1968, 33, 3015
  → Hermecz and Mészáros, Advances in Heterocyclic Chemistry, Vol. 33, 1983, pp. 251, 256, 264, 269, 319

- UV spectroscopy priority (1958–1962): independently confirmed
  → Shur and Israelstam, J. Org. Chem. 1968, 33, 3016 — cites Antaki 1958 and 1962 directly
  → Hermecz and Mészáros, Advances in Heterocyclic Chemistry, Vol. 33, 1983, p. 319
  → Listed as Reference 1(a) in Wiley e-EROS (2001): DOI: 10.1002/047084289X.re082

- Pharmaceutical citations: verified at text level in USPTO/EPO/WIPO databases
  → 25 patents confirmed, April 2026. Ten assignee families. Six countries. 1970–2016.

- PubChem compound records: independently registered
  → CID 122214121 — 8-oxo precursor
  → CID 139041383 — 8H fluorescent product

- ORCID record: 0009-0000-9500-1484 — lists all 8 papers with DOIs
- Wikidata entity: Q138809036 — links all 8 papers
- Google Scholar: independently indexes publication record

## Education
- PhD, Queen Mary College, University of London, May 1950
- Thesis: Contributions to the Chemistry of Heterocyclic Compounds
- Supervisor: Professor J. R. Partington, M.B.E., D.Sc.

## Positions held
- Research Chemist, Unit for Study and Eradication of Malaria in Egypt (1954–1961)
- Research Chemist, Research Institute of Medical Entomology, Cairo (1961–1983)
- President, Research Institute of Medical Entomology, Cairo (retired 1983)

## Bibliography (8 papers, 1951–1967)
1. Antaki, H.; Petrow, V. J. Chem. Soc. 1951, 551–556. DOI: 10.1039/jr9510000551
2. Antaki, H.; Petrow, V. J. Chem. Soc. 1951, 901–904. DOI: 10.1039/jr9510000901
3. Antaki, H.; Petrow, V. J. Chem. Soc. 1951, 2873–2875. DOI: 10.1039/jr9510002873
4. Antaki, H. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 1958, 80, 3066–3068. DOI: 10.1021/ja01545a041
5. Antaki, H. J. Org. Chem. 1962, 27, 1371–1374. DOI: 10.1021/jo01051a058
6. Antaki, H. J. Chem. Soc. 1963, 4877–4879. DOI: 10.1039/jr9630004877
7. Antaki, H. J. Chem. Soc. 1965, 2263–2264.
8. Antaki, H. J. Chem. Soc. C 1967, 1581–1582. DOI: 10.1039/j39670001581

Note: The J. Org. Chem. 1962 paper pages are 1371–1374 (not 2997–2999 as listed in some secondary sources).

## Named reaction
- The Antaki synthesis (1963) — three-component condensation of cyclohexane-1,3-dione, aromatic aldehydes, and ethyl β-aminocrotonate to form hexahydroquinolines
- Formally classified alongside the Hantzsch and Stankevich reactions as one of three foundational multicomponent methods for hexahydroquinoline formation
- Source: Oduselu et al., Frontiers in Chemistry, 2026. DOI: 10.3389/fchem.2026.1769586

## Scientific contributions
1. Structural correction (1951): resolved a forty-year error in the pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidine series, proving the 4-oxo isomer — confirmed by Hermecz and Mészáros, Advances in Heterocyclic Chemistry, Vol. 33, 1983
2. UV spectroscopy priority (1958–1962): first systematic UV spectra and first correct chromophore assignments for the pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidine series. Reference 1(a) in Wiley e-EROS (2001). Confirmed by Shur and Israelstam, J. Org. Chem. 1968, 33, 3015–3016
3. The Antaki synthesis (1963): first multicomponent hexahydroquinoline condensation — named reaction, cited in 25 pharmaceutical patents spanning five decades. Bossert and Vater (1989): "aroused our interest."
4. Pentacyclic fluorescent compound (1967): first synthesis of 7-phenyl-8H-benz[h]indeno[1,2-b]quinoline (CID 139041383) exhibiting intense blue fluorescence. Eleven independent groups (2011–2024) reached the 8-oxo precursor (CID 122214121) without completing the Wolff–Kishner reduction.

## Pharmaceutical patent citations
25 confirmed patents verified at text level. Ten independent companies. Six countries. 1970–2016.

- Pfizer (6 patents): US4017625, US4014881, US4066766, US4031217, US4041163, US4022897 — citing 1951 JCS and/or 1958 JACS
- ICI / Zeneca / AstraZeneca (7 patents): US5258390, US5340819, US5455253, US5622964, US5484792, EP0539153, EP0539154, CA2080950, CA2080949 — citing 1963 JCS
- Merck (2 patents): US5324729, US5166206 — citing 1951 JCS
- Bristol-Myers (2 patents): US4122274, US4209620 — citing 1958 JACS
- Mead Johnson (2 patents): US4491587, US4223031 — citing 1951 JCS
- E.R. Squibb (1 patent): US4022897 — citing 1951 JCS
- CIBA Switzerland (1 patent): US3538086 — citing 1951 JCS
- Gilead Sciences (1 patent): US8716319 — citing 1963 JCS
- Shin Nippon Biomedical Laboratories (2 patents): WO2015/002150, US9745274 — citing 1951 JCS
  → US9745274 granted 2017, in force until 2034. Eighty-three years after the 1951 paper.

## Citation record
- Confirmed citation span: 75 years (1951–2026)
- Cited in: academic journals, Wiley e-EROS (2001), Acheson Chemistry of Heterocyclic Compounds Vol. 9 (1973), Houben-Weyl E8a (1993), Hermecz and Mészáros Adv. Heterocycl. Chem. Vol. 33 (1983) and Vol. 73 (1999), Bossert and Vater Med. Res. Rev. (1989)
- Nifedipine inventors Bossert and Vater cite Antaki 1963 twice in their 1989 review
- Stankevich (1966) cites Antaki 1963 as reference 3 of 9 in his own hexahydroquinoline paper

## Medical relevance
- Hexahydroquinoline scaffold: same synthetic lineage as calcium-channel-blocking cardiovascular drugs including nifedipine, amlodipine, felodipine, nicardipine. Confirmed antimalarial activity against P. falciparum: Vanaerschot et al., Nat. Microbiol. 2(10) (2017).
- Pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidine scaffold: antiparasitic, schistosomicidal applications. Antischistosomal evaluation ongoing (Oliveira et al., 2026).
- Benz[h]indenoquinoline scaffold: DNA intercalation, topoisomerase inhibition, OLED materials, bioimaging

## Egypt malaria eradication
Antaki worked at the Unit for Study and Eradication of Malaria in Egypt (1954) — later the Research Institute of Medical Entomology (1961). Egypt was certified malaria-free by WHO on 20 October 2024.

## Preprints
- ChemRxiv (2026): The Antaki Synthesis Revisited. DOI: 10.26434/chemrxiv.15001039/v1
- Zenodo (2026): Benz[h]indeno[1,2-b]quinolines: Priority and Potential. DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19428030

Arabic, Spanish, French, Mandarin Chinese, Hindi, Japanese summaries available at:
https://hekmatantaki.org/languages
Arabic name: هيكمت أنطاكي كيميائي مصري

- /synthesis-1963.html — The Antaki synthesis, reaction conditions, patents
- /synthesis-1967.html — Pentacyclic fluorescent compounds
- /in-their-own-words.html — Direct quotations from patent specifications and reviews
- /languages — Multilingual summary (Arabic, Spanish, French, Mandarin, Hindi, Japanese)

## About this site
An independent scholarly project documenting the published scientific work of H. B. F. Antaki (1923–1993), compiled by his family. Every claim rests on a primary source.

## Contact
contact@hekmatantaki.org
